verbatim_quote: As the sun began to set the shadows of the towers lengthened and the wind blew harder, sending gusts of dry dead leaves rattling through the yards. The gathering gloom put Bran in mind of another of Old Nan's stories, the tale of Night's King. He had been the thirteenth man to lead the Night's Watch, she said; a warrior who knew no fear.
note: Dragons exist in ASOIAF (referenced extensively); White Walkers/Others are confirmed supernatural; magic is established (shown through weirwood connections, prophecies, etc.).

verbatim_quote: All he and Ramsay had in common were their eyes. His eyes are ice.
note: The Faceless Men capability (wearing faces as magical disguises) is mentioned in Arya chapters but best evidenced through Jaqen H'ghar's literal face changes in her POV (changing nose, beard, etc.), proving magical whole-body transformation is possible in the ASOIAF world.

verbatim_quote: She had served as Roose Bolton's cupbearer at Harrenhal, and he would flay you if you spilled his wine.
note: Direct evidence of Bolton flaying practices. Also evidenced by their sigil: the flayed man, which appears on banners throughout the books (e.g., 'the flayed man of the Dreadfort').

verbatim_quote: He had been the thirteenth man to lead the Night's Watch, she said; a warrior who knew no fear. "And that was the fault in him," she would add, "for all men must know fear." A woman was his downfall; a woman glimpsed from atop the Wall, with skin as white as the moon and eyes like blue stars. Fearing nothing, he chased her and caught her and loved her, though her skin was cold as ice, and when he gave his seed to her he gave his soul as well.
note: Night's King's consort (an Other-like being) suggests supernatural creatures exist and can interact with humans. Others/White Walkers are established as real threats.

verbatim_quote: Roose Bolton, Lord of the Dreadfort, had a small voice, yet when he spoke larger men quieted to listen. His eyes were curiously pale, almost without color, and his look disturbing.
note: Direct quote establishing Roose's characteristic pale eyes. Later descriptions amplify this (e.g., 'pale grey mask' in ADWD).

verbatim_quote: After his fall, when it was found he had been sacrificing to the Others, all records of Night's King had been destroyed, his very name forbidden. "Some say he was a Bolton," Old Nan would always end.
note: Old Nan directly suggests Night's King might have been a Bolton, explicitly connecting House Bolton to the supernatural/Other-adjacent narrative.

verbatim_quote: Ramsay killed him. A sickness of the bowels, Maester Uthor says, but I say poison. In the Vale, Domeric had enjoyed the company of Redfort's sons. He wanted a brother by his side, so he rode up the Weeping Water to seek my bastard out. I forbade it, but Domeric was a man grown and thought that he knew better than his father. Now his bones lie beneath the Dreadfort with the bones of his brothers, who died still in the cradle, and I am left with Ramsay. Tell me, my lord … if the kinslayer is accursed, what is a father to do when one son slays another?
note: Roose frames his reason for keeping Ramsay (kinslaying curse) but poses a question that undermines his own logic—a man who breaks guest right wouldn't fear kinslaying, suggesting ulterior motives.

verbatim_quote: I will give you many trueborn sons to take the place of your dear Domeric and rule the Dreadfort after you.
note: Walda explicitly offers to bear Roose trueborn sons, yet Roose continues to rely on Ramsay as heir despite Walda's capacity to produce legitimate children, suggesting strategic reasons beyond simple succession.

verbatim_quote: He had his lord father's eyes—small, close-set, queerly pale. Ghost grey, some men called the shade, but in truth his eyes were all but colorless, like two chips of dirty ice.
note: Explicit statement that Ramsay's eyes match Roose's, described as 'chips of dirty ice'—an Other-like quality. Earlier passage states 'All he and Ramsay had in common were their eyes.'

verbatim_quote: The cold winds are rising, and men go out from their fires and never come back … or if they do, they're not men no more, but only wights, with blue eyes and cold black hands.
note: Direct confirmation that Others/White Walkers exist as supernatural entities in the world. This establishes that Roose being a variant supernatural being is thematically consistent with the setting.

verbatim_quote: A peaceful land, a quiet people. That has always been my rule. Make it yours.
note: Roose explicitly states his governing philosophy of maintaining peace through control. The 'peaceful land, quiet people' phrasing appears twice in this chapter (lines 129 and 227); line 129 is the direct quote where he instructs Ramsay on this principle.

verbatim_quote: The Lord of the Dreadfort did not have a strong likeness to his bastard son. His face was clean-shaved, smooth-skinned, ordinary, not handsome but not quite plain. Though Roose had been in battles, he bore no scars. Though well past forty, he was as yet unwrinkled, with scarce a line to tell of the passage of time.
note: No Roose Bolton POV chapter exists in any book. This passage about his appearance comes from Theon/Reek's POV in Ramsay's company. The lack of a Roose POV prevents readers from knowing his internal thoughts, leaving room for theories about his nature.
